column2: A phone shows poor battery health after years of normal use. What repair direction is most appropriate?
column4: Plan for battery replacement rather than treating it as an app or charging cable problem.
column5:
Why

Poor health after many charge cycles points to battery aging. App usage and signal checks matter for sudden drain, but the stated evidence is long-term battery wear.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Battery Mobile Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone battery drains quickly only when the user works in a basement with weak cellular reception. What likely cause should you investigate before replacing the battery?
column3: Look for a condition that makes the device keep searching.
column4: Poor reception causing the phone to spend extra power searching for signal.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The location pattern matters. A weak signal area can create battery drain even when the battery itself is not the root problem.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Battery Cellular Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user's phone started losing battery rapidly after a new app was installed. What evidence source should you check first?
column3: Measure which software is using the battery before blaming the battery itself.
column4: Check the phone's app battery-usage information.
column5:
Why

Sudden drain after an app change should be tested with usage evidence. This is more targeted than replacing the battery or charger first.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Battery Apps Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone case is bulging and the battery appears swollen. What is the safest first action?
column3: Treat physical battery deformation as a safety issue.
column4: Stop using the device immediately and do not open or puncture the battery.
column5:
Real Help Desk

A swollen battery can be a fire risk. Normal troubleshooting stops until the device is handled through the proper replacement and disposal process.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Battery Safety Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A mobile phone has a broken screen but still responds to touch. What should you do before sending it for screen replacement?
column3: Protect what the user cannot easily replace.
column4: Back up the user's data first if the device is still usable.
column5:
Why

Screen repair can fail or require device replacement. A backup reduces the risk that a repair task becomes a data-loss incident.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Screen Backup Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user wants to keep using a phone with cracked glass until a replacement part arrives. What safety concern should you address?
column4: The broken glass can be sharp, so protect the surface temporarily and arrange repair.
column5:
Why

This card is not testing cosmetic damage. It tests the safety angle that a help desk technician should communicate when a screen is physically broken.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Screen Safety Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone will not charge after being carried in a pocket all day. What should you inspect first?
column3: Start where the cable physically meets the device.
column4: Inspect the charging port for debris, obstruction, or visible damage.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The charging path starts at the device interface. A simple obstruction can look like a failed battery or charger.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Charging Ports Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone will not charge, and the user's cable is frayed near the connector. What is the best next test?
column3: Use a safe replacement for the suspect part of the charging path.
column4: Try a known-good charging cable.
column5:
Why

A damaged cable should not be trusted or reused. A known-good cable isolates the cable before replacing the phone.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Charging Cables Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone still does not charge after you inspect the port and try a known-good cable. What part of the charging path should you verify next?
column3: Follow the power path away from the phone.
column4: Verify the power adapter or charging power source.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The adapter is a separate part of the charging chain. Checking it keeps the diagnosis evidence-based before concluding the device has failed.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Charging Power Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user's phone has poor cellular service only inside one concrete building. What should you test before replacing the phone?
column3: Use location as a troubleshooting variable.
column4: Test cellular connectivity in another location, such as outdoors or away from the building.
column5:
Why

Mobile signal is location-dependent. A location-only symptom should be tested as coverage or signal strength before it becomes a device-failure conclusion.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Cellular Connectivity Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A tablet has slow Wi-Fi only in one corner of the office. What network condition should you consider before blaming the tablet hardware?
column3: Think about what changes as the user moves around.
column4: Limited Wi-Fi range or local interference.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The one-location pattern points to the wireless environment. Tablet hardware is less likely when the symptom follows a physical area.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic WiFi Connectivity Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user spilled water on a phone that is still powered on. What should you do first?
column3: Prevent additional electrical damage before testing.
column4: Power down the phone and do not charge it.
column5:
Real Help Desk

Testing a wet device by charging or powering it can make the damage worse. Stop normal troubleshooting until the device is safe to handle.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield LiquidDamage Safety Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: After a liquid spill, what removable items should you take off only if the phone design allows it?
column3: Remove external pieces without forcing the device open.
column4: Remove the case, cards, back cover, or battery if they are designed to be removed.
column5:
Why

The goal is to reduce trapped liquid and power risk without creating more damage. Do not pry apart a sealed device as part of entry-level support.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic LiquidDamage Mobile Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A wet phone appears dry on the outside after a few hours. What should you avoid doing too soon?
column3: Outside dryness does not prove the inside is safe.
column4: Avoid powering it on or connecting it to power too soon.
column5:
Exam Trap

The safe decision is patience, not a quick power-on test. Moisture inside the device can still cause damage after the exterior looks dry.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic LiquidDamage Safety Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone overheats whenever one navigation app is running. What should you check before assuming the battery is bad?
column3: Look for the workload that appears with the heat.
column4: Check whether the app is using excessive CPU or battery.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Overheating can be caused by workload. App usage evidence helps separate a software/resource issue from a battery or hardware failure.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Overheating Apps Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone overheats while sitting on a dashboard in direct sunlight. What should you do before hardware troubleshooting?
column3: Remove the obvious heat source first.
column4: Move the device out of direct sunlight and let it cool.
column5:
Why

Environmental heat can trigger shutdowns or overheating symptoms. Correct the environment before treating the phone as failed.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Overheating Environment Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A mobile device screen is on, but touch input is not responding. What simple first isolation step should you try?
column3: Start with a reversible step that resets the device state.
column4: Restart the mobile device.
column5:
Why

A restart can clear a frozen mobile interface before repair or replacement is considered. Exact key combinations vary by device, so the card tests the support decision rather than model-specific buttons.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Touchscreen Mobile Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone charging port is visibly bent and known-good charging parts do not help. What should you do next?
column3: Visible physical damage changes the support path.
column4: Escalate for hardware repair or replacement instead of continuing cable swaps.
column5:
Real Help Desk

Mobile ports are often not simple modular field repairs. Document the visible damage and test results before handoff.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Ports Charging Escalation Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user's phone shows unexpected pop-ups and unfamiliar apps. What should you run to test the malware theory?
column3: Use the suspicious symptoms to choose a way to test the theory.
column4: Run an approved mobile security app or scanner.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Unusual apps and pop-ups are malware clues. The card tests the evidence-based next step without naming a vendor-specific tool.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Malware Mobile Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A mobile device has high CPU use, overheating, and excessive battery drain with no obvious user activity. What threat category should you consider?
column3: The symptoms are not limited to battery hardware.
column4: Possible mobile malware.
column5:
Why

Malware can show up as resource, heat, data, or battery symptoms. The answer is a theory to test, not a final diagnosis.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Malware Battery Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A tablet registers touches slightly away from where the user taps. What software-based fix should you try if the device supports it?
column3: Align the input system with where the user actually touches.
column4: Run touch calibration.
column5:
Why

Cursor drift is an input-alignment symptom. Calibration is a targeted software step before assuming the screen assembly is failed.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Touchscreen Calibration Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone cannot install a new app and reports there is not enough space. What should you check first?
column4: Check available storage and remove unnecessary data if needed.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The error already points to storage. Do not troubleshoot the app store or network first when the device says space is the blocker.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Apps Storage Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A mobile app download never starts, but the device has plenty of free storage. What should you verify next?
column3: The app still has to come from somewhere.
column4: Verify network connectivity.
column5:
Why

App installation requires access to an external service. After storage is ruled out, connectivity becomes a focused next check.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Apps Connectivity Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A required app says it is not compatible with the current mobile OS version. What should you check?
column3: Compare the app requirement with the device software level.
column4: Check for an OS update or an updated app version that supports the device.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Compatibility is a different problem from storage or network access. The next step is to verify whether supported software versions exist.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Apps Compatibility Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A mobile app store keeps failing after storage and connectivity check out. What account or store-state check should you try?
column3: Look at the service state rather than the app being installed.
column4: Check store cache where applicable, or account sign-in state, then sign out and back in if appropriate.
column5:
Why

Store cache on supported platforms or credentials can block installation even when storage and network are fine. This card keeps the target to one account/store-state decision.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Apps Credentials Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A powered tablet stylus suddenly stops responding. What should you check first?
column3: Start with the simplest accessory condition.
column4: Check the stylus battery or charge level.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Powered accessories fail when they are discharged. This comes before re-pairing or replacing the stylus tip.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Stylus Power Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A stylus is charged, but the tablet no longer sees it as connected. What should you check next?
column3: Confirm the wireless relationship between the accessory and tablet.
column4: Check the Bluetooth connection and try re-pairing the stylus.
column5:
Why

Once power is ruled out, pairing is the next likely path for a wireless stylus. The card tests the accessory connection decision.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Stylus Bluetooth Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A charged and paired stylus skips or scratches on the screen. What physical part should you inspect?
column4: Inspect the stylus tip for wear or damage.
column5:
Why

If power and pairing are fine, the stylus itself may have a worn contact point. This is a targeted accessory check, not a full tablet replacement decision.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Stylus Hardware Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone is slow and has not been updated or restarted in months. What least-invasive action should you try first?
column3: Start with reversible software maintenance.
column4: Update the OS and apps as appropriate, then restart the device.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Outdated software and long uptime can degrade behavior. Updating and restarting are safer first steps than replacing hardware.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Performance Updates Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A phone is slow and frequently warns that storage is almost full. What should you address first?
column4: Free up storage by removing unnecessary data or apps.
column5:
Why

Low storage can contribute to slowdowns and crashes. The symptom gives a specific target before broader performance troubleshooting.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield Performance Storage Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A tablet slows down after many apps have been left open in the background. What should you try before replacing the tablet?
column3: Reduce active resource pressure first.
column4: Close unnecessary background apps.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Background apps can consume memory and processing resources. Closing them is a low-risk test before hardware escalation.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Performance Apps Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: An older phone becomes slow only when running a newly released app. What should you consider before diagnosing a device fault?
column3: Compare the app's expectations with the device's age.
column4: The app may not support the older hardware well.
column5:
Why

Newer apps may assume newer hardware capabilities. That is different from a random hardware failure and should be documented as a compatibility concern.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Performance Compatibility Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: You must escalate a liquid-damaged phone. What liquid-exposure evidence should you include in the ticket?
column3: Capture evidence the next technician cannot infer later.
column4: Document the liquid exposure details and any Liquid Contact Indicator status.
column5:
Real Help Desk

Good handoff records what happened, what was powered or charged, and whether an indicator showed exposure. This helps the next technician avoid unsafe retests.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.53; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic HighYield LiquidDamage Documentation Escalation Source::Messer-v170

column2: Before escalating a suspected damaged mobile charging port, what tested-path details should you document?
column3: Preserve which parts of the charging path were already ruled out.
column4: Document the visible port condition and the results of known-good cable and power-adapter tests.
column5:
Why

Escalation is more useful when it includes both physical evidence and isolation results. The next technician should not need to repeat basic charging-path tests.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.54; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.4
column10: A+::220-1201::5.4 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingMobileDevices Basic Ports Documentation Escalation Source::Messer-v170
